coredata.dc:title: Hollow-spherical composites of Polyaniline/Cobalt Sulfide/Carbon nanodots with enhanced magnetocapacitance and electromagnetic wave absorption capabilities
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Chuanjun
Zhang, Xiang
Liu, Jian
Jin, Feng
Liu, Jichang
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Hollow-spherical composites of polyaniline/cobalt sulfide/carbon nanodots (PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T) have been synthesized by in situ polymerization under an applied magnetic field (MF) of 0.5T. As a control, PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites have been synthesized without a MF. Both composites acting as electrodes present obvious magnetocapacitances at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 while the electrochemical cell tested under an external MF of 0.5T. Notably, PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites show larger magnetocapacitances than PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites at different scan rates from 5 to 100mVs−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results indicate that MF can reduce charge transfer resistance at electrode/electrolyte interface. More importantly, PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites show a much stronger electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorbing capability than PANI/CoS/CDs-0T in the range of 2–18GHz which is attributed to an increased dielectric loss and a magnetic loss in low frequency range of 2–12.5GHz. MF-induced ferromagnetic nanodomains of Co2+ clusters in the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites increase the complex permittivity and create more interfacial polarizations or the Maxwell–Wagner effect, which leads to increased dielectric loss. Compared with PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites with diamagnetic behaviour, MF-induced weak ferromagnetism of CoS in the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites has caused additional magnetic loss. This work provides an efficient way for modulating electrochemical or electromagnetic properties of inorganic/polymer nanocomposites by employing an external MF.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyaniline
Elelctromagnetic wave absorption
Magnetocapacitance
Magnetic field
Hollow-spherical

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurements of magnetocapacitance and electromagnetic wave absorption

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Environmental-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

University Natural Science Research Project of Anhui Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All of the co-author thank Prof. Yuhua Shen (College of chemistry and chemistry and chemical engineering Anhui University, China) for helping us to measure EDX spectra of samples. This work was financed by the 211 project of Anhui University, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51272002 ), the University Excellent Youth Talents Foundation of Anhui province ( 2013SQRL066ZD ) and the Key Laboratory of Environmental-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province , Anhui University. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: synthesis procedure of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and the control sample of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T.

SEM images of (a) PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T (the inset is the corresponding TEM image) and (b) PANI/CoS/CDs-0T; (c) EDX spectrum of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T; (d) XRD patterns of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T.
FT-IR spectra of PANI-0.5T, PANI/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T.

(a) Magnetic hysteresis curves of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T; (b) CV curves of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T; CV curves of (c) PANI/CoS/CDs-0T and (d) PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T tested with/without an applied MF.

CV curves at different scan rates of the PANI/CoS/CDs-0T performed (a) without a MF and (b) with a MF; CV curves at different scan rates of the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T performed (c) without a MF and (d) with a MF.
Nyquist plots of (a) PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and (b) PANI/CoS/CDs-0T (inset: the magnified images of Nyquist plots of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T, respectively).
Reflection losses of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T in paraffin with a layer thickness of 3mm.
(a) Real and (b) imaginary parts of permittivities of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T; (c) Real and (d) imaginary parts of permeabilities of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T.
Typical Cole–Cole semicircles of (a) PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and (b) PANI/CoS/CDs-0T.

head.ce:title: Hollow-spherical composites of Polyaniline/Cobalt Sulfide/Carbon nanodots with enhanced magnetocapacitance and electromagnetic wave absorption capabilities

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optc-electronic Information Acquisition and Manipulation Ministry of Education, Anhui University, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optc-electronic Information Acquisition and Manipulation Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hollow-spherical composites of polyaniline/cobalt sulfide/carbon nanodots (PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T) have been synthesized by in situ polymerization under an applied magnetic field (MF) of 0.5T. As a control, PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites have been synthesized without a MF. Both composites acting as electrodes present obvious magnetocapacitances at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 while the electrochemical cell tested under an external MF of 0.5T. Notably, PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites show larger magnetocapacitances than PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites at different scan rates from 5 to 100mVs−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results indicate that MF can reduce charge transfer resistance at electrode/electrolyte interface. More importantly, PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites show a much stronger electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorbing capability than PANI/CoS/CDs-0T in the range of 2–18GHz which is attributed to an increased dielectric loss and a magnetic loss in low frequency range of 2–12.5GHz. MF-induced ferromagnetic nanodomains of Co2+ clusters in the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites increase the complex permittivity and create more interfacial polarizations or the Maxwell–Wagner effect, which leads to increased dielectric loss. Compared with PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites with diamagnetic behaviour, MF-induced weak ferromagnetism of CoS in the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites has caused additional magnetic loss. This work provides an efficient way for modulating electrochemical or electromagnetic properties of inorganic/polymer nanocomposites by employing an external MF.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demand for high power energy storage and resources has aroused great interest in the development of supercapacitors due to its high power supply, long-term stability and environmental friendliness [1]. Among various materials used for supercapacitors, polyaniline (PANI), as a typical conducting polymer, has become not only a promising material of pseudo-capacitors [2–5], but also one of the most attractive electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding materials [6–9]. PANI owns many advantages such as electric conductivity, lightness, stability and ease of synthesis, however, it tends to swell and shrink during the charge/discharge process, which results in poor cycle stability of supercapacitors and low energy density because of its narrow potential window in aqueous electrolytes. Moreover, while against incident electromagnetic wave (EMW), PANI alone can only induces dielectric loss, the drawback of low EMW absorption restricts its application as EMI shielding material. Therefore, it is an efficient way to combine PANI with carbon material (a typical electric double layer (EDL) capacitor material) to achieve a better electrochemical performance as well as a long cycle stability [10]. For example, PANI/multi-walled carbon nanotubes composite was reported with a specific capacity of 440Fg−1 at 5mVs−1 and a capacitance retention of 93% after 1000 cycles [11]. And sandwich-like PANI/boron-doped graphene showed enhanced specific capacitances in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes [12]. Alternatively, some t ransitional metal oxideswith a potential window extended to negative potential, e.g., vanadium oxide, can be combined with PANI film and assembled into flexible, high energy density supercapacitor [13]. In particular, ternary composite of RGO/MnO2/PANI shows a large specific capacitance of 636.5Fg−1 in 1.0M Na2SO4 electrolyte and excellent cycling stability, owing to the optimized composite structure with more electroactive sites, fast ion and electron transfer, and strong structural integrity [14].

With regard to improving microwave absorption property of PANI, compared to preparing binary composites of PANI and carbon materials [15], a more general way is to construct heterostructured composites of PANI, carbon materials and magnetic nanoparticles, such as CNTs/PANI/Fe3O4 [16] and Co/C/PANI [17] endowed with both dielectric loss and magnetic loss. Recently, we reported a novel ternary composite of PANI/ferrocene/C-dots [18] that exhibited a remarkably enhanced microwave absorption property due to introduction of a new kind of mainly sp 2-hybridized carbon nanomaterial, namely, carbon nanodots (CDs).

More interestingly, Guo et al. reported magnetic field (MF) induced capacitance enhancement in graphene and magnetic graphene nanocomposites [19]. Further, they found that a small external MF of 0.072T could significantly influence the specific capacitances of the supercapacitor with fabric carbon/magnetic metal (iron, cobalt or nickel) oxides nanocomposites as flexible electrodes [20]. This phenomenon, named as magnetocapacitance, has been interpreted as a synergistic effect of three internal resistances: solution resistance in electrolyte solution, charge transfer resistance at electrode/electrolyte interface and leakage resistance in electric double layer region. MF-induced magnetohydrodynamic phenomena in bulk electrolyte and magnetoresistance (MR) behaviour of electrode are the main reasons for the tuned solution resistance and charge transfer resistance. These findings present a new method to enhance the conventional capacitance by simply applying an external MF.

On the other hand, it is known that an applied MF can dramatically affect chemical reactions, having effect on morphology and microstructure of the products [21]. Up to date, the methods involving MF-induced formation of ferro/ferrimagnetic, paramagnetic and diamagnetic nanomaterials with special structures have been exploited extensively and continuously in various synthesis systems, which has been reviewed by several groups [22–25]. In addition, MF can induce orientation to most organic polymer molecules, which makes the diamagnetic molecular chains aligned more orderly. In 1997, Cai et al. reported that PANI polymerized in a MF owned a higher degree of crystallinity as well as a more ordered morphology [26,27]. Recently, Ma et al. found that lignosulfonate-doped PANI nanoparticles prepared in the presence of a MF of 0.4T exhibited a much higher conductivity, solubility, and thermo-stability than the control sample prepared without a MF [28], which was due to the stretch and orientation effects of MF that caused a molecular conformation with more extended chains, and thus a larger conjugated system formed and the co-planarity of the molecular chains was also improved.

In the present study, a hollow-spherical composite of PANI, CDs and cobalt sulfide (CoS), named as PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T, was synthesized by in situ polymerization at room temperature under an applied MF of 0.5T. The reason why we chose CoS is because it is a semiconductor showing a weak ferromagnetism at room temperature as well as a high dielectric constant (ca. 8). To date, CoS has been widely used in solar cells, supercapacitors, counter electrodes and lithium-ion batteries [29–32]. Therefore, not only are the effects of MF on the formation of hetero-structured ternary composites and the resulting morphology and crystallinity worthy of study, furthermore, the possible influence of MF on specific capacitance and EMW absorption properties of the composites deserve further investigation.

Aniline (C6H7N), ammonium peroxydisulfate ((NH4)2S2O8), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), sodium hydrogen sulfide nonagydrate (Na2S·9H2O) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. Carmphorsulfonic acid (C10H16O4S) was supplied by Aladdin. Aniline was distilled before used, and other chemicals were used without further purification. All water used in this process is deionized water.

Briefly, 0.558g (0.006mol) of aniline and 0.2323g (0.001mol) of camphorsulfonic acid (CSA) were added into 50mL of deionized water under stirring at room temperature, then preheated at 30°C in water bath for 1h in order to make the solution dispersed evenly. After that, 1.3692g of ammonia peroxydisulfate (APS) dispersed in 50mL of deionized water was added to the mixture under. Finally, The reaction was kept for 24h under an applied magnetic field of 0.5T. After the reaction, the dark green solids were filtered and then washed with deionized water for several times, followed by drying overnight at 80°C. The obtained product was named as PANI-0.5T. As a control, PANI-0T was also synthesized without a MF.

The synthesis procedure of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T were shown in Scheme 1 . The nanocomposite samples were synthesized by a one-step facile method via in situ polymerization. In a typical synthesis of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T, 0.558g (0.006mol) of aniline (A), 0.2323g (0.001mol) of CSA, 50mg of carbon dots (CDs) and 0.1561g (0.002mol) of Na2S (B) was added into 50mL of deionized water under stirring at room temperature, and then preheated at 30°C in water bath for 1h in order to make the solution dispersed evenly. After that, 1.3692g of APS dispersed in 50mL of deionized water was added to the mixture while 0.4759g (0.002mol) of CoCl2·6H2O (C) was dissolved in 10mL of deinoized water. Subsequently, the aqueous solution of CoCl2 was dropped into the former solution slowly under an applied magnetic field (MF) of 0.5T. The reaction was kept for 24h. Finally, the dark green solids were filtered and then washed with deionized water for several times, followed by drying overnight at 80°C. The obtained product was named as PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T (A:B: C=3:1:1). As a control, PANI/CoS/CDs-0T (A:B: C=3:1:1) was also synthesized without a MF. In the meantime, PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T (A:B: C=2:1:1) and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T (A:B: C=4:1:1) and the corresponding control samples of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T (A:B: C=2:1:1) and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T were also prepared by varying the molar ratios of aniline to CoS from 3:1:1 to 2:1:1 and 4:1:1, respectively.

The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a NEXUS-870 spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were carried out on an X’Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu K radiation. The morphology of the products was observed on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL) and a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi). Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was performed by using Oxford Instruments, INCA x-act, 51-ADD0053. Elemental analyses were determined by a Leaman inductively coupled plasma (ICP) spectrometer. The static magnetic properties were measured with a PPMS-9 (Quantum Design) in a magnetic field up to 20kG at temperature of 300K. CV was measured by using a CHI600E of CH Instruments.

Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) was performed at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1 in 0.5M Na2SO4 aqueous solution recorded in a potential range from 0 to 1V. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out at the frequency range of 100 mHz to 100kHz. The measurements were carried out using a conventional three-electrode system (in a Zahner PP123 electrochemical workstation): (a) the composites coated glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, (b) a platinum electrode as the counter electrode, and (c) a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. To avoid a reduction in sensitivity, the working electrode was carefully polished with 0.03μm alpha alumina powder and ultrasonically rinsed in deionized water at the end of each cycle. All samples were dispersed into ethanol and then coated on the working electrode. In addition, magnetocapacitance measurements were performed with the electrochemical cell tested under an external MF of 0.5T.

The complex permeabilities, complex permittivities and the reflection losses of PANI, PANI/CDs, and PANI/CoS/CDs in paraffin with the same weight concentration of 30% and a layer thickness of 3mm were measured by using an Agilent E5071 vector network analyzer in the frequency range of 2–18GHz, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 1 (a), PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites are in a uniform morphology of micro-sized hollow spheres with navel-like holes. The corresponding TEM image (the inset in Fig. 1(a)) confirms their hollow structure. As a control, PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites show an irregular shape consisting of nanofibers and microspheres (Fig. 1(b)). In addition, an obvious difference of morphology also exists between the tube-like PANI-0.5T synthesized with a MF of 0.5T and the irregular-shaped PANI-0T synthesized without a MF (see Fig. S1). As previously reported, the most commonly generated nanostructure for PANI is the nanofiber when acids with bulky organic side groups such as CSA have been used as soft-templates or doping acids [33–36]. However, in the present case, an applied MF has contributed to forming PANI nanotubes instead of nanofibers.

In general, when aniline is oxidized under aqueous conditions of higher pH, ortho-coupled aniline is initially formed, and then oxidizes into phenazine-like moieties. The phenazine units might aggregate into a template-like structure that promotes nanotube growth [37,38]. An applied MF will supply excessive energy for the reaction that favours the oxidation of aniline into phenazine-like moieties, therefore nanotubes appear to be the major product of PANI-0.5T. Why the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites turn to be hollow microspheres remains unanswered. PANI hollow spheres have often been observed when oxidizing aniline in emulsion type polymerizations [39,40]. Our hollow spheres are typically microns in diameter, but the shells are in the order of tens of nanometers. Clearly, a MF-assisted self-assembly process occurred, in which aniline is oxidized by APS in the presence of CSA and then the oligomer generates at the first stage, as illustrated in Fig. S2. It is believed that coordination bonds between N atoms in aniline and Co2+ ions make oligomer-Co2+ salts aggregate into micelles and form the shell of the polymeric microsphere. In the meantime, the monomer droplets composed of aniline monomer acts as the core to supply monomer to the shell of micelles. As the reaction proceeds, aniline monomer in the monomer droplets is consumed and contributes to the growth of polymeric shell. Eventually hollow spherical PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites are formed.

The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum (Fig. 1(c)) indicates the co-existence of C, N, S and Co elements in PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites. It can be seen that the content of S is much higher than that of Co because large amount of sulfur-containing acid (CSA) was added as a dopant in the reaction with Na2S added as a reactant. Unfortunately, it can’t identify the accurate contents of Co and S because of semi-quantitative feature of EDX. Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) spectrum reveals that the mass content of Co element in the composite is about 2%. Fig. 1(d) shows the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T. No diffraction peaks of crystalline CoS can be observed, indicating amorphous phase of CoS in both samples. However, the former sample has a better crystallinity than the latter, which confirms the MF-induced effect on crystallinity of PANI.

All of the FT-IR signals of PANI have somewhat shift in that of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T (Fig. 2 ). The characteristic peak (1585cm−1) of quinone ring for PANI has shifted to 1575cm−1 (PANI/CDs-0.5T) and 1578cm−1 (PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T) [41–43]. Besides, the peak (1505cm−1) of benzenoid ring for PANI has shifted to 1500cm−1 (PANI/CDs-0.5T) and 1503cm−1 (PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T). In the meanwhile, the peak at 1298cm−1 (a feature peak of the conducting emeraldine salt form of PANI) has shifted to 1303cm−1, which suggests that a coordination bond between Co2+ and nitrogen atoms located in PANI backbone might be formed. The strong coordination between Co2+ and nitrogen atoms in PANI may be responsible for the amorphous state of CoS in both PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of magnetocapacitance and electromagnetic wave absorption
ce:section.ce:para: M-H loops, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), indicate that PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites exhibit a weak ferromagnetic property while PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites are diamagnetic at room temperature. Since paramagnetic Co2+ ions randomly disperse in the PANI/CDs matrix that is diamagnetic (as shown in Fig. S3), the entire PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites exhibit a diamagnetic behaviour. However, during the synthesis of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T under an applied MF, paramagnetic Co2+ ions were magnetized and their magnetic moments would align in parallel towards the same direction, then MF-induced ferromagnetic nanodomains of Co2+ clusters might be formed in the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites. Further, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed to evaluate electrochemical behaviour of these composites in a three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 3(b), PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites exhibit a superior capacitance to that of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T, which can be attributed to larger specific surface area as well as better crystallinity of hollow spherical PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites. More interestingly, a unique capacitance behaviour is expected when CV performed under an external MF that may change electron spin energy states during the electrochemical oxidation-reduction reaction [19]. Fig. S4 illustrated setup for magnetocapacitance measurement. As expected, Fig. 3(c)–(d) demonstrates obvious magnetocapacitances behaviour in both electrodes composed of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites at a scan rate of 100mVs−1. In addition, a cyclic voltammogram shows the fundamental difference of the current curves between static capacitors and pseudocapacitors. For an ideal double-layer capacitor, the current flow is reversed immediately upon reversing the potential yielding a rectangular-shaped of the voltammogram, with a current independent of the electrode potential. For double-layer capacitors with resistive losses, the shape changes to a parallelogram. In faradaic electrodes of pseudocapacitors, the electrical charge stored in the capacitor is strongly dependent on the potential, therefore, the voltammetry characteristics deviate from the parallelogram due to a delay while reversing the potential, ultimately coming from kinetic charging processes [44]. In our work, all of the PANI/CoS/CDs-0T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composite samples present more likely a pseudocapacitor behaviour than a double-layer capacitor, therefore, there is no good regtangular box shape plots no matter tested with or without an applied MF.

Notably, the latter shows larger magnetocapacitances than the former even at different scan rates from 5 to 100mVs−1 (Fig. 4 ). Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to investigate the redox (charge/discharge) processes of electrode materials and to evaluate their electronic and ionic conductivities with/without a MF. Fig. 5 shows the EIS curves of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T electrodes in a three-electrode system. Both EIS curves of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T include a high-frequency region (arc) and a low-frequency region (straight line). Herein, the left-handed high-frequency intercept with the X-axis represents the equivalent series resistance (R s), associated with the sum of the electrolyte solution resistance, the intrinsic resistance of active material and the contact resistance at the electrode–electrolyte interface [4]. The charge transfer resistance of electrode (R ct) can be calculated from the diameter of the semicircle in the high frequency region, which relates to interfacial processes of counterions through the electrode/electrolyte interface. Besides, the straight line at lower frequencies presents the diffusion behaviour of ions in the electrode pores [4,45]. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the R s values of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T electrode measured with or without an external MF are almost the same, which indicates that they have the same electronic transport ability no matter measured under a MF or not. PANI/CoS/CDs-0T electrode also has the same behaviour of R s when tested with/without a MF (Fig. 5(b)). However, the R ct of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T electrode measured with a MF is only 255.98Ω while R ct measured without a MF becomes a little bigger to 262.54Ω. In addition, the high frequencies region is magnified and embedded into the blank. As for the sample of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T, the steeper shape of its sloped line measured with a MF than measured without a MF indicates the faster diffusion of ions in electrolyte under an external MF [4]. In the meantime, the smaller R ct measured with a MF than that measured without a MF reveals that counterions with a higher rate pass the electrode/electrolyte interface into PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites electrode. Obviously, PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites measured with a MF show a smaller R ct than that measured without a MF, too (Fig. 5(b)). Therefore, EIS results indicate that an applied external MF can reduce the charge transfer resistance at electrode/electrolyte interface, which is probably because of magnetic nature of CoS in the PANI/CoS/CDs composite electrode, and thus lead to larger magnetocapacitances [20].

Moreover, the microwave absorption properties of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T in paraffin matrix are studied. As shown in Fig. 6 , the maximum reflection loss (R L) of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T is −12.5dB at 16.8GHz while that of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T achieves −24dB at 14GHz. Besides, the bandwidth at R L = −10dB in the former is only 0.64GHz whereas that in the latter becomes three-fold as wide as 1.92GHz. It has been demonstrated that PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T has a much stronger EMW absorbing capability than PANI/CoS/CDs-0T. Moreover, EMW absorption properties of PANI-0.5T, PANI/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T are also studied and presented in Fig. S5. Obviously, PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites show a stronger absorption than both of PANI-0.5T and PANI/CDs-0.5T in the same range of 2–18GHz. In addition, a comparison of the R L of other four composites in different molar ratios of A:B:C has been depicted as in Fig. S6. Compared with PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites (A:B:C=3:1:1), not only binary composites of PANI/CoS-0.5T but also other ternary composites of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T (A:B:C=2:1:1 and 4:1:1) are all weaker absorbers, which confirms a fact that the molar ratio of 3:1:1 (A:B:C) in feed is an optimum condition for the synthesis of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites.

The origin of the enhanced microwave absorption property is analyzed on the basis of electromagnetic parameters. Fig. 7 (a) shows the frequency dependence of the real parts (ε′) of the complex permittivities of both PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T in paraffin matrix. The ε′ values of the former are obviously higher than those of the latter in overall range of 2–18 GHz, which means more polarizations occurred in the former sample. The imaginary parts of permittivities (ε′′) of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T also show higher values than those of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T, which indicates a greater dielectric loss (Fig. 7(b)). Besides, the real and the imaginary parts of permeabilities (μ′, μ′′) of both samples are shown in Fig. 7(c)–(d), respectively. The μ′ values of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T are mostly higher than 1.0 below 8.5 GHz and then fluctuate about 1.0 owing to the MF-induced weak ferromagnetism, whereas μ′ values of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T keep relatively stable at 1.0 over the 2–18 GHz due to its diamagnetic property. Correspondingly, μ′′ values of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T are fluctuating within 0–0.32 but mostly higher than that of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T (ca. 0.05) over 2–18 GHz, which indicates a certain amount of magnetic loss from the former sample but no magnetic loss comes from the latter. Notably, μ′′ values of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T become negative above 12.5 GHz, which is likely to be a measurement error arising from the equipment.

Further, the dielectric and magnetic dissipation factors (tanδe
= ε′′/ε′, tanδμ
= μ′′/μ′) of both samples are calculated. As shown in Fig. S7(a), the dielectric tangent loss (tanδε ) of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T increases gradually from 0.15 to 0.25 with frequency increasing from 2 to 18GHz, however, the magnetic tangent loss (tanδμ ) is fluctuating within 0–0.32 below 12.5GHz and then decreasing to negative values in 12.5–18GHz. As a control, tanδε of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T is fluctuating within 0–0.1 over the range of 2–18GHz while tanδε is declining from 0.6 to −0.1 (Fig. S7(b)). Since minus value of tanδμ doesn’t make any sense, it can be concluded that the dielectric loss is the main contributor to the enhanced EMW absorption of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T as the magnetic loss is negligible in high frequency range (f >12.5GHz). Certainly, both a dielectric loss and a magnetic loss have made contributions to the EMW absorption of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T in low frequency range of 2–12.5GHz. The magnetic loss originates from MF-induced weak ferromagnetism of CoS in the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T, as demonstrated in Fig. 3(a). In contrast, only dielectric loss contributes to the EMW absorption of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T composites that are diamagnetic.

It is known that Debye dipolar relaxation is an important mechanism for a dielectric loss material to absorb microwaves. If the Debye relaxation process accounts for dielectric loss behaviour, the plot of ε′ versus ε′′ would be a single semicircle, generally denoted the Cole–Cole semicircle. Fig. 8 shows the ε′- ε′′ curves of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0T in paraffin. Several Cole–Cole semicircles are found for both samples, representing the existence of the Debye relaxation process, similar to previously reported CuS in PVDF matrix [46]. However, the Cole–Cole semicircles are distorted, suggesting that besides the Debye relaxation, other mechanisms such as Maxwell-Wagner relaxation and electron polarization also exist in these two composites [47,48]. The multiple interfaces in heterogeneous composites give rise to interfacial polarization or the Maxwell–Wagner effect due to accumulation of charges at the interfaces and the formation of large dipoles on particles or clusters [49]. Interfacial relaxation depends on the conductivity and permittivity of the constituents of the composite. Fig. S8 shows that both ε′ and ε′′ of PANI/CDs-0.5T are greater than those of PANI-0.5T due to introducing conductive graphitic CDs [18]. Compared with PANI/CDs-0.5T, a substantial increase of ε′ of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T can be observed by incorporation of highly dielectric CoS. Nevertheless, MF-induced ferromagnetic nanodomains of Co2+ clusters in the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T can greatly enhance Maxwell-Wagner relaxation as well as ionic orientation polarization, leading to higher ε′ and thus a greater dielectric loss than that of PANI/CoS/CDs-0T.

Additionally, similar phenomena have been observed in other binary or ternary composites such as PANI/CoS-0.5T and PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T (A:B:C=2:1:1 and 4:1:1), as shown in Fig. S9. All of the results have demonstrated that the increased ε′ and thus greater dielectric loss are major contributor to the enhanced EMW absorption capability of PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites. Besides, magnetic loss arising from the MF-induced ferromagnetism of the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T also takes effect in low frequency range.

In summary, hollow-spherical PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites have been synthesized by in situ polymerization under an applied MF of 0.5T. It has been demonstrated that MF can not only change the morphology, crystallinity and magnetic property of the ternary composites, futhermore, endow the PANI/CoS/CDs-0.5T composites a better electrochemical and electromagnetic behaviour than PANI/CoS/CDs-0T, such as presenting a larger magnetocapacitance as well as an enhanced electromagnetic wave absorption capabilities. We believe that an applied MF can play an important role in fabricating new functional inorganic/polymer nanocomposites with more special properties and modulating these functionalities.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All of the co-author thank Prof. Yuhua Shen (College of chemistry and chemistry and chemical engineering Anhui University, China) for helping us to measure EDX spectra of samples. This work was financed by the 211 project of Anhui University, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51272002), the University Excellent Youth Talents Foundation of Anhui province (2013SQRL066ZD) and the Key Laboratory of Environmental-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Anhui University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.03.210.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
